overall_summary: Exelon, a major transmission owner serving PJM territory, supports large load interconnection reform but opposes the ANOPR's apparent lean toward direct 100% participant funding of upgrade costs. Exelon argues that Transmission Security Agreements (TSAs) paired with deposit requirements better protect existing customers from cost shifts than assigning upgrade costs up front, which would allow large loads to escape ongoing transmission charges. Exelon also warns against modeling the effort on Order No. 2003, citing its failure to expedite generation interconnection, and calls for broader attention to supply adequacy.

quotes.text: Large load interconnection frameworks must not only aim to expediently, fairly, and reliably interconnect new customers, these processes must also take care not to exacerbate affordability challenges facing the country.
quotes.concern: Interconnection rules must protect affordability for existing customers, not just speed for new ones.

quotes.text: Overstated demand expectations can, and likely will, trigger costly system upgrades or new facility construction to serve that anticipated demand. If expected demand never materializes, infrastructure and capacity costs can end up borne by other customers.
quotes.concern: Speculative load projections can shift stranded upgrade costs onto existing customers.

quotes.text: These agreements are designed to ensure that a contribution to the transmission revenue requirement is made by large load customers commensurate with their requested end-use retail demand.
quotes.concern: TSAs require large load customers to cover their share of transmission revenue requirements.

quotes.text: The design of the TSA is intended to weed out speculative customer behavior while protecting other customers in the event a large load project realizes less than its promised load.
quotes.concern: TSAs deter speculation and shield other ratepayers if a large load underperforms.

quotes.text: while there is surface appeal to proposals to directly allocate 100 percent of transmission upgrades driven by new large load customers, such an approach may not achieve the goal of avoiding undue cost shifts and may put other customers at considerable risk.
quotes.concern: 100% participant funding of upgrades could paradoxically increase cost shifts to other customers.

quotes.text: If new large loads are made to be responsible for the costs of upgrades as a requirement to interconnect, pursuant to the Commission's prohibition on 'and' pricing and concerns over undue discrimination, those same loads will undoubtedly maintain that they are not required to pay separately for transmission service (i.e., for network integration transmission service, the 'NITS' rate).
quotes.concern: Requiring upgrade payment up front will lead large loads to claim exemption from ongoing NITS charges.

quotes.text: By spreading the revenue requirement for the transmission system generally over an increasing number of billing units--a.k.a. 'increasing the denominator'--it reduces what other customers pay for transmission.
quotes.concern: Including large loads in the general rate base lowers per-unit costs for all other customers.

quotes.text: The more large loads are carved out to pay separately for certain transmission infrastructure, the less they will pay for the overall grid and the more other customers will pay.
quotes.concern: Carving out large loads from general transmission rates harms all other ratepayers.

quotes.text: Exelon does not believe that established principles of rolled-in cost allocation need to be challenged to achieve the aims of the ANOPR and, indeed, an approach that avoids doing so will be both most effective and most expeditious.
quotes.concern: Rolled-in cost allocation is preferable to participant funding and is consistent with ANOPR goals.

quotes.text: such an approach creates challenging dynamics like those we have seen in the generator interconnection space. For example, this approach likely benefits earlier loads that identify favorable interconnection points, only for later large load customers to trigger significant upgrades
quotes.concern: Direct upgrade assignment creates queue-gaming incentives similar to problems seen in generation interconnection.

quotes.text: Generation capacity is not being developed fast enough to replace retiring resources and meet these expanded demands on the system.
quotes.concern: Supply adequacy is falling behind load growth, threatening affordability and reliability.

quotes.text: Exelon supports generator interconnection reforms that recognize the value in 'bring your own generation' ('BYOG') approaches, in which generation dedicated to serve specific large load customers comes online with financial support and commitments from those customers.
quotes.concern: Exelon backs BYOG arrangements but wants safeguards against cost shifts to other customers.

quotes.text: treatments of hybrid configurations need to recognize that associated generators and loads fully use and benefit from the transmission system.
quotes.concern: Hybrid load-generation configurations must pay for their full use of the transmission network.

quotes.text: Order No. 2003, as the Commission is aware, took more than five years to progress from the initial ANOPR (October 2001) to the end of the appeal process (January 2007). Even on an accelerated timeline, Commission action here could extend compliance well into the next presidential administration, and the jurisdictional issues are likely to be vigorously litigated as well.
quotes.concern: Using Order No. 2003 as a template risks years of delay through litigation and compliance proceedings.

quotes.text: it has been 24 years since the Commission commenced the proposed rulemaking that led to Order No. 2003 and we are still working to resolve the shortcomings of that rule.
quotes.concern: Order No. 2003 is a cautionary precedent, not a model, given its persistent failures.

bins.name: Oppose Direct Upgrade Cost Assignment
bins.description: Exelon opposes requiring large loads to pay 100% of transmission upgrade costs up front. It argues this approach invokes the 'and' pricing prohibition, letting large loads escape ongoing NITS charges, and ultimately shifts more cost onto other customers than rolled-in allocation would. TSAs are presented as a superior alternative that keeps large loads in the general rate base.

bins.name: Rolled-In Allocation Preferred
bins.description: Exelon contends that direct assignment of upgrade costs violates the 'and' pricing prohibition and creates perverse queue-shopping incentives mirroring problems in the generator interconnection queue. Rolled-in allocation keeps large loads contributing to the full network, benefiting all ratepayers through a larger denominator.

bins.name: TSAs and Deposits Over Participant Funding
bins.description: Exelon supports Transmission Security Agreements, which function as take-or-pay transmission revenue commitments, combined with expanded retail deposit requirements. This combination deters speculative requests and protects existing ratepayers without removing large loads from the shared transmission revenue base.

bins.name: TSA Model as Reform Template
bins.description: Exelon describes its TSA structure in detail, including a ten-year take-or-pay commitment to the transmission revenue requirement and collateral security. It presents this model as ready to scale and as a better fit than participant funding for protecting customers while enabling rapid interconnection.

bins.name: And-Pricing Prohibition Risk
bins.description: Exelon warns that assigning upgrade costs to large loads as an interconnection condition will trigger claims under the Commission's 'and' pricing prohibition, allowing those loads to avoid paying NITS rates. The result would be a net transfer of transmission cost burden from large loads to other customers.

bins.name: Queue-Gaming Incentives from Direct Assignment
bins.description: Direct cost assignment for upgrades replicates the generator interconnection queue's worst dynamics: early movers secure cheap interconnection points, later arrivals trigger costly upgrades that cycle benefits back to future entrants, and the uncertainty drives speculative behavior and point-of-interconnection shopping.

bins.name: Supply Adequacy Must Accompany Load Reform
bins.description: Exelon argues that interconnection reform alone is insufficient because generation capacity is not keeping pace with retiring resources and rising demand. PJM in particular faces structural barriers to new supply, and the Commission should advance reforms that also speed generator interconnection.

bins.name: Generation Shortfall Threatens Reliability
bins.description: Exelon flags that PJM's deference to merchant markets has left an accountability gap in supply adequacy. It urges states to allow utilities to propose and authorize new generation builds to backstop the market, with the Commission supporting faster generator interconnection for state-prioritized resources.

bins.name: Hybrid Resources Must Pay for Full Network Use
bins.description: Exelon supports BYOG and hybrid configurations but insists any rules must require hybrid loads and generators to pay for their full use of the transmission network. Allowing hybrid configurations to avoid full transmission charges would shift costs to existing customers and degrade system reliability planning.

bins.name: BYOG Support with Cost-Shift Guardrails
bins.description: Exelon endorses behind-the-meter or co-located generation models where large loads bring their own dedicated generation, but conditions that support on ensuring full transmission cost responsibility for hybrid facilities.

bins.name: Flexibility in Generation Arrangements
bins.description: Exelon supports flexible approaches including BYOG and hybrid structures that let large loads and their generators share interconnection points, as long as cost and reliability obligations are fully preserved.

bins.name: Order 2003 Analogy Creates Legal Risk
bins.description: Exelon warns that modeling large load interconnection rules on Order No. 2003 is legally and practically risky. Load interconnection differs from generation interconnection in fundamental ways, jurisdictional disputes are likely, and Order No. 2003 itself took over five years to finalize and still has not resolved underlying problems after 24 years.

bins.name: Order 2003 Is Not a Success Model
bins.description: Exelon argues that Order No. 2003 did not succeed in expediting generation interconnection, took years to litigate, and its shortcomings are still being corrected. Replicating that approach for load interconnection risks the same years-long delay and would be counterproductive to the ANOPR's urgency goals.
